Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. At 28, I've got a lot going on outside of university, so I'd study whenever I could squeeze it in. I'd usually wait until I felt the pressure building, then sit down with my notes and readings for a few hours. It wasn't organized at all—more like survival mode. I'd read through materials, make notes by hand, and try to absorb what I could in whatever time I had.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd cram in 20 hours because deadlines were looming. I never really established a consistent schedule—it just felt impossible with everything else I was juggling. Deadlines were basically my only real motivator to sit down and focus.
Q3: Usually it was panic, honestly. I'd log in when I realized an assignment was due soon or when I needed to check what I'd missed. Sometimes I'd get a notification or remember during a random moment that I should probably see what's happening in my courses. It wasn't a habit—more like a reaction to feeling scared about falling behind.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, but then life would get in the way and I'd disappear for a while. The peer support group I was connected with helped remind me sometimes, but even then, it never felt natural or automatic to check in regularly.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me because I learn best through reading and writing. I could download PDFs, print them out, and work through them at my own pace with a highlighter. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful too—seeing other students' questions sometimes clarified things I was confused about. Videos were okay but I preferred text.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the videos most of the time. I know I watched some, but sitting through long lectures online just didn't work for me. I'd also skip optional readings or supplementary materials because I was just trying to get through the essentials. When you're scared of failing and short on time, you triage what you can actually handle.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help me pass, I'd do it. Anything that felt vague or disconnected from assessment just got pushed aside. I needed to see immediate relevance because I didn't have the luxury of exploring things just for interest.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. They'd make me feel guilty for not doing them, but I knew I couldn't manage everything. Required activities got done because they had to—there was accountability. Optional stuff just added to my anxiety without giving me any credit, so I mostly avoided it to preserve my mental energy.
Q9: Absolutely. I did most of my actual studying offline. I'd download readings, print them out, and work through them with handwritten notes. I have notebooks full of summaries and key points that never showed up in any system. I also used some external websites and YouTube videos when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely studying but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours rereading my printed notes, rewriting concepts to memorize them, or working through practice problems on paper. None of that leaves a trace online. My actual learning happened away from the screen most of the time.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading once, download it, and never open it online again. I preferred having physical copies or files on my laptop that I could access without internet. It felt more manageable to study from my own organized folders than to navigate back through the LMS every time.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt overwhelming and I just couldn't engage. When stress piled up from other areas of my life—work stuff, personal issues—the course became something I dreaded rather than something I could manage. My attendance dropped because showing up felt impossible when I was already running on empty.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big one—being 28 and managing multiple responsibilities means I'm often exhausted. When I was tired or stressed, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at it without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities constantly pulled me away, and confusion about course content made me avoid things rather than tackle them.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd open materials, scroll through them, maybe watch part of a video, but my mind would be elsewhere. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out due to exhaustion or worry.
Q15: I postponed things all the time, especially when they seemed difficult or when I was scared of not understanding them. The fear of doing something wrong made me avoid starting. I knew it was self-sabotaging, but the anxiety of confronting hard material was sometimes worse than the anxiety of leaving it until later.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in, download everything, and work frantically. Without deadlines, I'd drift. The structure they provided was the main thing keeping me from completely disengaging from the course.
Q17: Feedback helped when I got it, but honestly, I didn't always check it right away. When I did read feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder on the next assignment. Other times it just made me feel more scared about my performance. The peer support helped more than formal feedback—having someone check in felt more personal.
Q18: The structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having seven online courses meant I was constantly juggling different deadlines and platforms. The course structure gave me a roadmap, but when I fell behind, it became a source of panic rather than guidance. I needed more flexibility than the structure allowed.
Q19: The reading-heavy format actually suited my learning style, which helped. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made engagement harder. When there were too many steps or confusing navigation in the LMS, I'd get frustrated and give up. Simpler, clearer organization would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, trying to figure things out, but feeling too overwhelmed or confused to actually complete work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just lost, searching for something that makes sense, or procrastinating by looking busy.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I did most of my real studying offline, so my LMS activity was low even when I was learning. My exam scores and assignment completion were decent because I put in work—just not in ways the system could track. Low activity doesn't mean low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the anxiety that keeps you from engaging even when you're trying. They're a shallow measure of something much deeper and more complicated.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and understanding the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it would mean feeling connected to the content and confident enough to participate. Right now, engagement feels more like survival—doing enough to pass while managing fear and stress. True engagement would feel less desperate.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I've been throughout this course, or how much effort it takes just to open my laptop some days. It can't capture the peer support conversations that kept me going, or the hours I spent with printed notes trying to make sense of things. My engagement was real, just mostly invisible.
